---
title: "NFDI4Objects KG — Ogham Sites on a Map"
format: live-html
engine: knitr
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
---

{{< include ../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

## About this notebook

This notebook takes the same
[NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph](https://graph.nfdi4objects.net/)
dataset that the companion notebook `n4okg-ogham-sites-county-live.qmd`
visualises as counts, and plots each individual
[Ogham site](http://ontology.ogham.link/OghamSite) on two interactive
[Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/) maps. The first map shows
individual sites as county-coloured markers with pop-up details;
the second shows the same points styled by stone count — marker
size and colour both encode how many stones were disclosed at each
site, making regional concentration visible at a glance.

It is part of an Open Educational Resource series on knowledge
graphs and linked open data, and is designed to stand on its own:
you do not need to have read any other notebook in the series to
follow along. A local-Python variant of this notebook is available
as `n4okg-ogham-sites-map.ipynb`.

### Why this dataset?

Ogham sites from the N4O KG's
[`collection/9`](https://graph.nfdi4objects.net/collection/9) are a
good fit for a map teaching example because:

- Each site has a **GeoSPARQL `hasGeometry`/`asWKT` literal** with
  explicit WKT point coordinates — a common, well-specified
  encoding that generalises to many other knowledge graphs.
- The **stone-count-per-site** metadata is rich enough for two
  different map treatments: a categorical view coloured by county,
  and a density-weighted heatmap.
- The sites concentrate in just two Irish regions, so the map is
  geographically interesting without being sparse.

### Data-context notes

Two subtleties about coordinates that we handle here and that
generalise to other GeoSPARQL-using KGs:

- **WKT is `lon lat`, Leaflet is `lat, lon`.** Getting this the
  wrong way round is one of the most common mistakes when plotting
  spatial data — the kind of bug where everything "works" but all
  your points end up in the wrong hemisphere. Our parser swaps the
  order explicitly.
- **Some endpoints uppercase the `POINT` keyword, some don't.**
  Wikidata writes `Point(...)`, the N4O KG writes `POINT(...)`.
  Our parser is case-insensitive, which is a reasonable defensive
  posture when working across endpoints.

A third point worth mentioning: N4O KG data is contributed by many
projects under shared infrastructure, each with its own ontology.
The Ogham ontology (`oghamonto:`) is separate from the NFDI4Objects
core vocabulary. This is normal for domain-rich knowledge graphs
and one of the reasons SPARQL is expressive: you can mix
vocabularies freely in a single query.

### Tooling notes

Rendering an interactive map in a browser-only notebook is
slightly unusual, because the Python libraries most people know for
this (`folium`, `geopandas`, `contextily`) do not work well in
Pyodide: `folium` expects to write HTML files, `geopandas` has
heavy binary dependencies (GDAL, GEOS, PROJ), and `contextily`
needs server-side HTTP. The local `.ipynb` companion happily uses
`folium`; the browser variant instead builds an HTML snippet with
embedded Leaflet JavaScript and returns it as the cell's output.

There is one additional browser-specific constraint worth knowing
about: `quarto-live` runs Python inside a **Web Worker**, which has
*no DOM access*. That means `js.document`, `js.eval()` targeting the
DOM, and any direct manipulation of page elements from Python will
fail with `ReferenceError: document is not defined`. The working
pattern is the one used below: Python *builds* an HTML string
containing the map container and the Leaflet-initialisation script,
then returns it as the cell value. `quarto-live` inserts that HTML
into the page on the main thread, where the browser has full DOM
access and runs the embedded JavaScript normally.

::: {.callout-note}
On first load, your browser downloads the Python runtime (Pyodide, ~10 MB)
and the Leaflet library (~150 KB). Please allow a moment for these
to initialise.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
The N4O KG is a research prototype. If this notebook fails to load
data with a *network error*, the endpoint may be temporarily
unreachable or may not allow cross-origin browser requests from
this page's domain. The local `.ipynb` companion is not affected
by this and is always a reliable fallback.
:::

## Step 1 — Defining the SPARQL query

The query below asks the N4O KG for every `oghamonto:OghamSite`,
its label, its GeoSPARQL geometry, the `County` it lies in, and a
count of catalogued Ogham stones (`oghamonto:OghamStone_CIIC`)
disclosed at that site. We group by site — one row per site with
its aggregated stone count.

Two notes on query design that generalise to other knowledge-graph
queries:

- **`geo:hasGeometry/geo:asWKT`** uses SPARQL property-path
  notation to traverse two predicates in a single triple pattern.
  It is equivalent to introducing an intermediate variable, but
  shorter.
- **`COUNT(DISTINCT ?stone)`** — not just `COUNT(?stone)`. A stone
  could in principle be linked through more than one path;
  `DISTINCT` makes sure we count each stone once.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: setup
import json
import re
import urllib.parse
from pyodide.http import pyfetch
import pandas as pd

SPARQL_ENDPOINT = "https://graph.nfdi4objects.net/api/sparql"

SPARQL_QUERY = """
PREFIX oghamonto: <http://ontology.ogham.link/>
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX geo: <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#>
SELECT ?item ?label ?geo ?county (count(distinct ?stone) as ?count) WHERE {
  ?item <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type> <http://ontology.ogham.link/OghamSite> .
  ?item rdfs:label ?label .
  ?item geo:hasGeometry/geo:asWKT ?geo .
  ?item oghamonto:within ?c .
  ?c a oghamonto:County .
  ?c rdfs:label ?county .
  ?stone oghamonto:disclosedAt ?item .
  ?stone a oghamonto:OghamStone_CIIC .
} GROUP BY ?item ?label ?geo ?county ORDER BY DESC(?count)
"""

async def fetch_sparql(query, endpoint=SPARQL_ENDPOINT):
    """Retrieve SPARQL results from a SPARQL 1.1 endpoint (browser-compatible)."""
    full_url = endpoint + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(
        {"query": query, "format": "json"}
    )
    response = await pyfetch(
        full_url,
        headers={"Accept": "application/sparql-results+json"},
    )
    data = await response.json()
    return data["results"]["bindings"]

# Case-insensitive WKT parser: handles both 'Point(...)' (Wikidata) and
# 'POINT(...)' (N4O KG) and variations; also tolerates an optional SRID prefix
# like '<http://www.opengis.net/def/crs/OGC/1.3/CRS84> Point(...)'.
_WKT_POINT_RE = re.compile(r"point\s*\(\s*([-+\d.eE]+)\s+([-+\d.eE]+)\s*\)",
                           re.IGNORECASE)

def parse_wkt_point(wkt):
    """Parse a WKT Point literal 'POINT(lon lat)' (any case) into (lat, lon)."""
    if not wkt:
        return (None, None)
    m = _WKT_POINT_RE.search(wkt)
    if not m:
        return (None, None)
    try:
        lon, lat = float(m.group(1)), float(m.group(2))
        return (lat, lon)
    except ValueError:
        return (None, None)

print("✓ Helpers defined. Run the next cell to load the data.")
```

## Step 2 — Loading the data

The `parse_wkt_point` helper defined above converts each
`POINT(lon lat)` literal into a `(lat, lon)` tuple. Note the
ordering: WKT is `lon lat`, but Leaflet (and most mapping libraries)
expect `lat, lon`. Getting this the wrong way round is one of the
most common mistakes when plotting spatial data — the kind of bug
where everything "works" but all your points end up in the wrong
hemisphere.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: load-data
results = await fetch_sparql(SPARQL_QUERY)

rows = []
for r in results:
    lat, lon = parse_wkt_point(r.get("geo", {}).get("value"))
    rows.append({
        "item":      r["item"]["value"],
        "label":     r["label"]["value"],
        "county":    r["county"]["value"],
        "count":     int(r["count"]["value"]),
        "latitude":  lat,
        "longitude": lon,
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows).dropna(subset=["latitude", "longitude"])
assert not df.empty, "⚠ No records with coordinates returned — check query."
print(f"✓ {len(df)} Ogham sites loaded across {df['county'].nunique()} counties "
      f"(total stones: {df['count'].sum()}).")
df.head()
```

## Step 3a — Map with county-coloured markers

We build the first map as an HTML string in Python and return it as
the cell's value via a `_repr_html_`-reprable object. `quarto-live`
inserts the HTML on the main thread, where the browser happily runs
the embedded `<script>` with full DOM access.

This first map loads only core Leaflet, plus the
[`Leaflet.fullscreen`](https://github.com/Leaflet/Leaflet.fullscreen)
plugin for the maximise-to-fullscreen button under the zoom controls.

A [layer control](https://leafletjs.com/examples/layers-control/) in
the top-right corner (expand it by hovering or tapping) offers four
base layers — OpenStreetMap as the default, the Humanitarian OSM
style, Esri satellite imagery, and Esri's world terrain base — and
one overlay *per county*, each with its colour swatch in the label.
Unchecking a county hides just its markers, so you can isolate
regions interactively. All provider URLs and attributions are taken
from the
[leaflet-providers registry](https://github.com/leaflet-extras/leaflet-providers).

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: render-markers-map
assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

import uuid

# --- 1. Assign a colour to each county (reused across both maps) ---------
palette = [
    "#e6194B", "#3cb44b", "#4363d8", "#f58231", "#911eb4",
    "#42d4f4", "#f032e6", "#469990", "#9A6324", "#800000",
    "#808000", "#000075", "#e6beff", "#fabed4", "#aaffc3",
    "#fffac8", "#dcbeff", "#ffd8b1", "#a9a9a9", "#ffe119",
]
counties = sorted(df["county"].unique())
county_colors = {c: palette[i % len(palette)] for i, c in enumerate(counties)}

# --- 2. Prepare records as plain JSON-serialisable dicts -----------------
markers = [
    {
        "lat":    float(row["latitude"]),
        "lon":    float(row["longitude"]),
        "name":   row["label"],
        "county": row["county"],
        "count":  int(row["count"]),
        "color":  county_colors[row["county"]],
        "url":    row["item"],
    }
    for _, row in df.iterrows()
]

# Sorted county names paired with colours — JS will build one layer per county
county_list = [{"county": c, "color": county_colors[c]} for c in counties]
markers_payload = json.dumps({"markers": markers, "counties": county_list})

# --- 3. A unique id so re-runs don't collide with previous renders -------
markers_map_id = f"ogham-markers-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
markers_data_var = f"__oghamMarkersData_{markers_map_id.replace('-', '_')}"

# --- 4. Build the HTML + JS string ---------------------------------------
markers_html = f"""
<div id="{markers_map_id}" style="height:560px;width:100%;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px;margin-top:8px"></div>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css" />
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.css" />
<script>window["{markers_data_var}"] = {markers_payload};</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {{
    const mapId = "{markers_map_id}";
    const data = window["{markers_data_var}"];

    // Load Leaflet.fullscreen dynamically AFTER core Leaflet is ready.
    // Same pattern as the heatmap plugin: avoid the race where a parallel
    // <script> tag evaluates before L is defined.
    function loadFullscreenPlugin(cb) {{
        if (typeof L !== 'undefined' && L.control.fullscreen) return cb();
        if (typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(() => loadFullscreenPlugin(cb), 50);
        const s = document.createElement('script');
        s.src = 'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.js';
        s.onload = cb;
        document.head.appendChild(s);
    }}

    let initAttempts = 0;
    const MAX_ATTEMPTS = 40;  // 40 × 50ms = 2 seconds total grace period

    function init() {{
        const el = document.getElementById(mapId);
        if (!el || typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(init, 50);
        if (el._leaflet_id) return;

        // Fullscreen plugin is optional; wait briefly, then proceed without it
        const hasFullscreen = !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen);
        if (!hasFullscreen && initAttempts < MAX_ATTEMPTS) {{
            initAttempts++;
            return setTimeout(init, 50);
        }}

        // --- Base layers (one active at a time) ------------------------
        // URLs and attributions taken from the leaflet-providers registry:
        // https://github.com/leaflet-extras/leaflet-providers
        const osm = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors'
        }});
        const osmHOT = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors, '
                + 'Tiles style by <a href="https://www.hotosm.org/" target="_blank">Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team</a> '
                + 'hosted by <a href="https://openstreetmap.fr/" target="_blank">OpenStreetMap France</a>'
        }});
        const esriImagery = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 18,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: Esri, i-cubed, USDA, USGS, AEX, GeoEye, '
                + 'Getmapping, Aerogrid, IGN, IGP, UPR-EGP, and the GIS User Community'
        }});
        const esriTerrain = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Terrain_Base/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 13,  // Esri caps World_Terrain_Base at zoom 13
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: USGS, Esri, TANA, DeLorme, and NPS'
        }});

        // --- Overlays: one layer per county ---------------------------
        // Each county gets its own L.layerGroup so it can be toggled
        // independently. The marker colour comes from the same palette
        // used elsewhere in the notebook.
        const countyLayers = {{}};
        const allBounds = [];
        for (const c of data.counties) {{
            countyLayers[c.county] = L.layerGroup();
        }}
        for (const m of data.markers) {{
            const marker = L.circleMarker([m.lat, m.lon], {{
                radius: 6, color: m.color, weight: 1.5,
                fillColor: m.color, fillOpacity: 0.75
            }}).bindPopup(
                '<b>' + m.name + '</b><br>' +
                'County: ' + m.county + '<br>' +
                'Ogham stones: ' + m.count + '<br>' +
                '<a href="' + m.url + '" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Site URI &#8599;</a>'
            );
            marker.addTo(countyLayers[m.county]);
            allBounds.push([m.lat, m.lon]);
        }}

        // --- Create map, show OSM + all county layers by default -------
        // Build an HTML legend swatch inside each overlay label so the
        // layer-control doubles as a colour key.
        const initialLayers = [osm];
        const overlays = {{}};
        for (const c of data.counties) {{
            initialLayers.push(countyLayers[c.county]);
            const swatch = '<span style="display:inline-block;width:10px;height:10px;'
                + 'background:' + c.color + ';border-radius:50%;'
                + 'margin-right:6px;vertical-align:middle"></span>';
            overlays[swatch + c.county] = countyLayers[c.county];
        }}

        const mapOptions = {{
            center: [53.0, -8.0], zoom: 7,
            layers: initialLayers
        }};
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            mapOptions.fullscreenControl = true;
            mapOptions.fullscreenControlOptions = {{ position: 'topleft' }};
        }}
        const map = L.map(mapId, mapOptions);
        if (allBounds.length) map.fitBounds(allBounds, {{ padding: [30, 30] }});

        const baseLayers = {{
            "OpenStreetMap": osm,
            "OpenStreetMap.HOT": osmHOT,
            "Esri.WorldImagery": esriImagery,
            "Esri.WorldTerrain": esriTerrain
        }};
        // collapsed:true → Leaflet default, expands on hover / tap
        L.control.layers(baseLayers, overlays, {{ collapsed: true }}).addTo(map);

        setTimeout(() => map.invalidateSize(), 200);
    }}

    loadFullscreenPlugin(init);
}})();
</script>
"""

class _Html:
    def __init__(self, s): self._s = s
    def _repr_html_(self): return self._s

_Html(markers_html)
```

## Step 3b — Map styled by stone count

The second map shows the *same* sites as Step 3a, but here the
stone count per site drives the visual encoding: each marker's
**radius is proportional to the count**, and its **colour follows
a continuous `viridis` gradient** from low (dark purple) to high
(bright yellow). The two encodings reinforce each other — busy
sites stand out clearly, whereas in a density heatmap they would
blur together with their neighbours.

A design note on why this is not a heatmap: heatmaps are best at
answering *"where are points concentrated in space?"*, not
*"where are the large values?"*. Since our dataset has
well-separated sites with very uneven counts (one site with 30+
stones, many with just one or two), a density heatmap weighted by
count drowns the low-count sites in a hazy background and makes
the high-count ones barely distinguishable. Proportional circles
are the honest visualisation here.

The map re-uses the four base layers from Step 3a and adds the
same fullscreen control. Because the overlays are SVG
`CircleMarker`s (not a canvas heatmap), no special redraw
handling is needed on fullscreen toggle.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: render-count-map
assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."
assert 'markers' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the previous cell (Step 3a) first."

count_map_id = f"ogham-counts-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
count_data_var = f"__oghamCountData_{count_map_id.replace('-', '_')}"

# The busiest site drives the radius scale. Small radii on low counts
# keep every site visible; large radii on high counts immediately
# draw the eye to where the action is.
max_count = max(m["count"] for m in markers)

# Passing the full marker records plus max_count — JS handles radius
# and colour mapping so that the legend and the markers stay in sync.
count_payload = json.dumps({"markers": markers, "max_count": max_count})

count_html = f"""
<div id="{count_map_id}" style="height:560px;width:100%;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px;margin-top:8px"></div>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css" />
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.css" />
<script>window["{count_data_var}"] = {count_payload};</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {{
    const mapId = "{count_map_id}";
    const data = window["{count_data_var}"];

    // Load Leaflet.fullscreen dynamically after core Leaflet is ready.
    function loadFullscreen(cb) {{
        if (typeof L !== 'undefined' && L.control && L.control.fullscreen) return cb();
        if (typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(() => loadFullscreen(cb), 50);
        const s = document.createElement('script');
        s.src = 'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.js';
        s.onload = cb;
        document.head.appendChild(s);
    }}

    // Viridis colormap, 10 stops sampled from matplotlib's canonical scheme.
    const VIRIDIS = ['#440154', '#482878', '#3E4A89', '#31688E',
                     '#26828E', '#1F9E89', '#35B779', '#6DCD59',
                     '#B4DE2C', '#FDE725'];

    // Discretise counts into N bins with equal-width ranges starting at 1.
    // Returning a bin index lets markers and legend share the same colour
    // logic — no "continuous markers, discrete legend" mismatch.
    const N_BINS = 5;

    function binRanges(maxCount) {{
        // Bin 0: [1, hi0], bin 1: [hi0+1, hi1], ..., bin N-1: [..., maxCount]
        const step = Math.ceil(maxCount / N_BINS);
        const ranges = [];
        for (let i = 0; i < N_BINS; i++) {{
            const lo = i * step + 1;
            const hi = Math.min((i + 1) * step, maxCount);
            ranges.push([lo, hi]);
        }}
        return ranges;
    }}

    function binIndex(count, maxCount) {{
        const step = Math.ceil(maxCount / N_BINS);
        return Math.min(Math.floor((count - 1) / step), N_BINS - 1);
    }}

    function binColor(idx) {{
        // Pick colour from the *middle* of each bin's share of the viridis
        // gradient, so bins are clearly different but not at the extremes.
        const t = (idx + 0.5) / N_BINS;
        const vi = Math.min(Math.floor(t * VIRIDIS.length), VIRIDIS.length - 1);
        return VIRIDIS[vi];
    }}

    // Radius: sqrt scaling so that *area* (not radius) is proportional to
    // count — perceptually this is what the eye judges. 5 px floor so the
    // smallest site is still clickable; 22 px cap so the biggest doesn't
    // overwhelm the map.
    function radiusFor(count, maxCount) {{
        const t = Math.sqrt(count / maxCount);
        return 5 + t * 17;
    }}

    let attempts = 0;
    const MAX_ATTEMPTS = 40;

    function init() {{
        const el = document.getElementById(mapId);
        if (!el || typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(init, 50);
        if (el._leaflet_id) return;

        const hasFullscreen = !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen);
        if (!hasFullscreen && attempts < MAX_ATTEMPTS) {{
            attempts++;
            return setTimeout(init, 50);
        }}

        // --- Base layers (same four as Step 3a) ------------------------
        const osm = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors'
        }});
        const osmHOT = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors, '
                + 'Tiles style by <a href="https://www.hotosm.org/" target="_blank">Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team</a> '
                + 'hosted by <a href="https://openstreetmap.fr/" target="_blank">OpenStreetMap France</a>'
        }});
        const esriImagery = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 18,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: Esri, i-cubed, USDA, USGS, AEX, GeoEye, '
                + 'Getmapping, Aerogrid, IGN, IGP, UPR-EGP, and the GIS User Community'
        }});
        const esriTerrain = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Terrain_Base/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 13,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: USGS, Esri, TANA, DeLorme, and NPS'
        }});

        // --- Overlay: one CircleMarker per site ------------------------
        const countLayer = L.layerGroup();
        const allBounds = [];
        for (const m of data.markers) {{
            const bi = binIndex(m.count, data.max_count);
            L.circleMarker([m.lat, m.lon], {{
                radius:      radiusFor(m.count, data.max_count),
                color:       '#333',
                weight:      1,
                fillColor:   binColor(bi),
                fillOpacity: 0.85
            }}).bindPopup(
                '<b>' + m.name + '</b><br>' +
                'County: ' + m.county + '<br>' +
                'Ogham stones: <b>' + m.count + '</b><br>' +
                '<a href="' + m.url + '" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Site URI &#8599;</a>'
            ).addTo(countLayer);
            allBounds.push([m.lat, m.lon]);
        }}

        const mapOptions = {{
            center: [53.0, -8.0], zoom: 7,
            layers: [osm, countLayer]
        }};
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            mapOptions.fullscreenControl = true;
            mapOptions.fullscreenControlOptions = {{ position: 'topleft' }};
        }}
        const map = L.map(mapId, mapOptions);
        if (allBounds.length) map.fitBounds(allBounds, {{ padding: [30, 30] }});

        const baseLayers = {{
            "OpenStreetMap": osm,
            "OpenStreetMap.HOT": osmHOT,
            "Esri.WorldImagery": esriImagery,
            "Esri.WorldTerrain": esriTerrain
        }};
        const overlays = {{
            "Sites (colour / size = stone count)": countLayer
        }};
        L.control.layers(baseLayers, overlays, {{ collapsed: true }}).addTo(map);

        // --- Colour-scale legend in the bottom-right -------------------
        // One row per bin, labelled with the range it covers. Marker
        // colours come from the exact same binColor() function, so the
        // legend matches what's on the map.
        const legend = L.control({{ position: 'bottomright' }});
        legend.onAdd = function() {{
            const div = L.DomUtil.create('div');
            div.style.cssText = 'background:rgba(255,255,255,0.92);'
                + 'padding:8px 12px;border-radius:4px;'
                + 'font:12px/1.5 system-ui,sans-serif;'
                + 'box-shadow:0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.2)';
            const ranges = binRanges(data.max_count);
            let html = '<b>Stones per site</b><br>';
            // Highest bin at the top of the legend
            for (let i = N_BINS - 1; i >= 0; i--) {{
                const [lo, hi] = ranges[i];
                const label = (lo === hi) ? ('' + lo) : (lo + '–' + hi);
                html += '<span style="display:inline-block;width:14px;height:14px;'
                    + 'background:' + binColor(i) + ';'
                    + 'border:1px solid #333;border-radius:50%;'
                    + 'margin-right:6px;vertical-align:middle"></span>'
                    + label + '<br>';
            }}
            div.innerHTML = html;
            return div;
        }};
        legend.addTo(map);

        setTimeout(() => map.invalidateSize(), 200);
    }}

    loadFullscreen(init);
}})();
</script>
"""

_Html(count_html)
```

## Step 4 — Exploring the data

The cells below are a free playground — filter the DataFrame by
county, compute per-county aggregates, or extend the query
yourself. Remember: in this notebook *one row = one site*, with a
`count` column holding the number of Ogham stones disclosed there.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: explore
assert 'df' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

# Example: list every Ogham site from a given county, with coordinates
# and stone counts. Change the filter below to explore other counties.
county_filter = "Kerry"

subset = (
    df[df["county"] == county_filter]
      [["label", "count", "latitude", "longitude"]]
      .sort_values("count", ascending=False)
      .reset_index(drop=True)
)
print(f"Ogham sites in {county_filter}: {len(subset)} "
      f"(total stones: {subset['count'].sum()})")
subset
```

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: explore-stats
assert 'df' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

# Per-county summary: number of sites, total stone count, and the
# geographic centroid of the sites. The centroid is a quick sanity
# check — it should fall inside the county on the map.
summary = (
    df.groupby("county")
      .agg(
          n_sites=("item", "nunique"),
          n_stones=("count", "sum"),
          mean_lat=("latitude", "mean"),
          mean_lon=("longitude", "mean"),
      )
      .sort_values("n_stones", ascending=False)
)
summary
```

---

*This notebook is part of the Open Educational Resources of
[NFDI4Objects](https://www.nfdi4objects.net/).*
